video_title: American Murder: Gabby Petito | Official Trailer | Netflix
comment_text:
Today, I watched the documentary on Gabby Petito's tragic case. I mourn the loss of a life-loving young woman who, until her final moments, clung to the man she loved, believing he was her safe harbor—unaware he would ultimately take her life.
​However, several aspects of the investigation are deeply upsetting and frankly surprising. The handling of the initial domestic dispute by the Moab police was highly unprofessional. The officer's casual attitude, and his comment comparing the situation to his own marriage while laughing, were completely inappropriate, especially given the clear signs of distress and potential assault. While Gabby admitted to initiating the physical contact, her fainting and overall deplorable state should have prompted a more thorough and empathetic response. The police had a duty to investigate the situation more rigorously, as she did not appear to be genuinely aggressive.
​The most baffling element remains the police's subsequent dealings with Brian Laundrie and his parents. I cannot comprehend how law enforcement failed to pursue a proper investigation or compel Brian to cooperate. Perhaps if they had forcibly interrogated him from day one, much more could have been revealed about Gabby's fate. Even after Brian's suicide, his parents should have been immediately questioned under duress. The contents of his mother's text message are, in my view, deeply unsettling and require further scrutiny.
​As someone who is recently emerging and still recovering from a toxic relationship, I feel a profound connection to Gabby's story. I sincerely hope that all women never underestimate the value of their lives or sacrifice their self-esteem for a partner who does not appreciate them. Justice for all victims of domestic violence.

comment_text: I just recently watched this doc. It's well worth the watch as it contains tons of videos and photos that I'd not seen before. One of my takeaways after watching their (her's, really) video logs is how phony the couple's relationship seemed. Simply acting happy all the time doesn't mean you are. It means you are trying really hard to convince other people that you are. He seems sullen and withdrawn, even when smiling, and comes across as a very unwilling participant in her postings. She comes across as shallow, vacuous, and attention seeking. It seems to have little to do with "living off the grid" and "van life", and more about just 'look at me, subscribe, donate, and approve of me!" If you were to stick any two people in a van for a few months, I'd be surprised if there wasn't some serious conflict going on. This is not to excuse anything he did, of course. I am just very distrustful of anyone, regardless of what they are doing or where they are living, who posts every moment of their life online. It always comes off as pandering and disingenuous.

video_title: American Murder: Gabby Petito | Official Trailer | Netflix
comment_text:
Here after watching the Netflix documentary.
As I watched the documentary, I remembered this happened during the pandemic. This couple took off on a van life journey during peak Covid time. Idk if anyone remembers but one of the things that was reported consistently during Covid time and lockdown was the rise of DV incidents and mental health crisis and suicides. 
During Covid, abusive partners were forced together due to lockdown and isolation, increasing the rate of abuse. 
Living a Van life with or without a pandemic is a tricky situation for new and unassuming individuals, even so for young couples couples, who’re thrown together in this little van with minimal resources  and under stressful conditions , navigating unknown circumstances. 
It can break even the coolest cucumbers. 
Gabby and Brian must’ve started getting on each other’s nerves through the journey and without really having anyone else to turn to, or a chance to cool off. . 
In this case Gabby was with a covert narcissist, little did she know that each time she was upset with him, he was secretly nursing a broken ego, looking for an opportunity to get even with her.
What I’m trying to say is, never get into a situation where you lose touch with your immediate family and close friends. Never lose sight of reality, no matter how much someone tries to manipulate you.
The minute you pick up on a red flag, you run!

comment_text:
*For one, Gabby was way too hot for this dude.* He literally looks like a child predator and looks 40 yet isn’t even 30. 
I’ve seen people in the comments saying Netflix is exploiting this for money but I think they’re bringing awareness to more women worldwide who will see this. There’s so many women in abusive relationships right now who are watching this documentary right now who need to see it so they can learn how to read the signs and know it’s ok to get away from the guy and they’re not alone in what they’re going through... 
He doesn’t complete you, he destroys you and treats you like a possession, not a person… 
This girl ran into the wrong type of guy. I’ve seen these hippie type of guys use the Jedi mind trick on chicks who were total a$$holes to those women too. And to make up for what they did they’d do some hippie gesture like give flowers and a cheesy poem that a 5 year old could write. Ladies, if you see an absolute dark side that really makes you scared then he turns it off like a light switch, run! And you never look back. RIP Gabby…

UPDATE: just finished the documentary… I’ve seen this story covered by YouTubers but I always end up falling asleep before I know what happened at the end and wow… Even in this dude’s last moments before ending himself he still lied about what actually happened… He couldn’t tell the truth in the end, imagine how this dude was throughout their entire relationship…
     He’s literally like every hippie dude I’ve ever encountered in my life when they interact with chicks. They lie and make themselves seem like these peace loving mellow mannered people yet that’s very far from who they really are. I met this couple who were hippies but they co-owned a yoga, palates and exercise company together and they were really into God. Well, come to find out, when they first met he was one of her students and he looked completely different. This guy was very smart and had a good education but he was definitely a shady guy. He had a habit of making people offended then acting as if them being offended was for no reason at all and would explain everything away perfectly. A master manipulator. That’s actually how he became co-owner of HER business. He totally morphed himself into what she would like. A hippie (he wasn’t at first) guy who looked like Jesus. He grew his beard and hair out long and dressed totally different. Then he’d slowly start moving himself into her life and home. She was older than him by at least 15 years although she looked very young for her age but you could tell she was older but she was very immature for her age too. Easily manipulated. He took full advantage of that. He started making her feel like she was stupid so he could control her more and make her feel like she needed him. She even made him co-owner of her business… 
To make a long story short, many people around them, myself included heard that he was hitting her behind the scenes. People were telling her that he’s basically using her and manipulating her for his own gain. Which was true. This dude was basically broke when he met her and people think he only went to palates class to find a woman to prey on but lo and behold, the person he was looking for was the teacher. I tried to warn her too before turning that friendship loose but it fell on deaf ears… The guy really did a Jedi mind trick on her and totally embedded himself like a tick into her life. 
There are different forms of abuse, ladies. It’s not always physical. It can be mental and emotional abuse too which hurts just as bad. You might love him, his schmeckle might be amazing but is it worth your sanity or your life? No. Get out while you can and make sure you have a net to land on when you jump out. Have people to protect you, always record unwanted interactions with that person, document everything and make sure you always have people around as witnesses and protectors.
Even if this helps one person, mission accomplished.

UPDATE: Normally I’d feel bad for saying something like this since Brian’s family basically helped him, I don’t feel bad. 
When I seen his sister and her husband I couldn’t stop laughing, lol. They both had no necks and were built and even looked the same, lol. RIP Gabby…

video_title: American Murder: Gabby Petito | Official Trailer | Netflix
comment_text: All around horrible tragedy. I'm glad her parents were able to get some sort of, "closure", if you even want to use a word like that, which is never sufficient when talking about your child. I really wish we knew what set it off that day in the van. Despite people getting offended by a legitimate question, I'm wondering who started in on who. Brian didn't seem like the type to aggressively start engaging anyone in an argument, and at the same time, seemed implosive. He seems more like someone who'd walk away from one. Unless something triggered him to do what he did. I'm sure it was a combination of finding out about her texting with her ex bf, essentially discussing plans on how to leave Brian, the fight we all have seen on body camera weeks before, and him getting kicked to the curb literally and metaphorically, the last night leading up to the murder, when she told him to go sleep outside in the elements. This is not in any way or form, victim bashing. What he did to her is absolutely egregious and disgraceful. His actions shortly after, as well as his parent's, are also extremely egregious and downright deplorable. I'm merely theorizing what set the spark off right before he decided that he was going to do what he did. I'm leaning towards more of the implosive and on impulse and emotions theory. He may have hit her and freaked when he realized he either killed her, or that the injury was so bad, she would have needed immediate medical attention. Who knows? Not only did he have absolutely no right to take her life, but he had no right to take his own shortly thereafter, which I'm sure is going to eat away at the family for the rest of their lives. So sorry ❤

Most interesting observation to be made here...

She grows up around some form of turmoil - her biological parents divorced.  Who knows what she saw between them before the divorce was finalized.   His parents are still together but wacky - based on what they did to support him after the crime.  I'm assuming he saw some odd stuff growing up as well.

They bond over similar negative stuff they've likely seen in their homes growing up, then they repeat it by refusing to leave a toxic relationship after it already proved that it wasn't healthy & never could be.

People repeat what they grew up around & can't help themselves when they get caught up in it.  It's just a shame that this rule applies when you experienced negative things growing up (& not only when you saw good things growing up).

comment_text:
Gabby Petito's tragic death is a devastating example of domestic violence, domestic assault, and homicide. The evidence gathered throughout the investigation confirms that Gabby was a victim of domestic abuse at the hands of her fiancé, Brian Laundrie. Throughout their relationship, Gabby's loved ones noticed signs of emotional manipulation and control, which are common indicators of domestic abuse.

The couple's road trip across the United States, which was supposed to be a fun adventure, turned into a nightmare for Gabby. Witnesses reported seeing Brian being physically and emotionally abusive to Gabby during their trip. For example, a witness called the police after seeing Brian and Gabby get into a physical altercation at a restaurant in Utah. These incidents, combined with Gabby's own words and texts, provide a heartbreaking insight into her experiences.

The autopsy report confirmed Gabby's cause of death as manual strangulation, throttling, and blunt force injuries, ultimately ruling her death a homicide(He strangled her with his hands around her neck squeezing really hard and he beat her to death). The investigation revealed Brian's culpability in Gabby's tragic demise. It's essential to acknowledge the devastating impact of domestic violence and hold perpetrators accountable for their actions. Brian's actions led to Gabby's tragic death, and it's crucial to recognize him as the perpetrator, not the victim.

Brian Laundrie's subsequent suicide does not change the fact that he was the perpetrator of domestic violence and homicide. His actions led to Gabby's tragic death, and it's essential to hold him accountable. Domestic violence is a serious issue that affects individuals from all walks of life, and it's crucial to recognize the warning signs and offer support to those affected. Brian's suicide does not absolve him of his responsibility for Gabby's death.

In remembering Gabby, we must continue to raise awareness about domestic violence and work towards creating a society where victims feel safe seeking help. By acknowledging the reality of Gabby's case and the role Brian played in her tragic death, we can honor her memory and work towards preventing similar tragedies in the future. If you or someone you know is experiencing domestic abuse, there are resources available to help, such as the National Domestic Violence Hotline (1-800-799-7233).

video_title: American Murder: Gabby Petito | Official Trailer | Netflix
comment_text:
On August 12, 2021, Gabby Petito and Brian Laundrie were involved in an altercation in Moab, Utah, which led to a police response. Body camera footage shows Gabby visibly shaken and crying, with scratches on her face and neck and arms. She told officers that Brian had grabbed her face and pushed her during an argument, which is consistent with behaviors exhibited by perpetrators of domestic violence.

The autopsy report confirmed that Gabby Petito's death was a homicide, caused by manual strangulation. Her body was discovered on September 19, 2021, in Bridger-Teton National Park, Wyoming, near where she and Brian Laundrie had been camping. The investigation revealed that Brian Laundrie had purchased a backpack and other gear in the days leading up to Gabby's disappearance, suggesting that he may have planned to flee the area.

The FBI's investigation, including a thorough analysis of Brian Laundrie's phone records, emails, and other digital evidence, suggests that he was responsible for Gabby's death. Furthermore, Brian Laundrie's own writings, discovered in his notebook, included statements that were interpreted by investigators as admissions of guilt. While Brian Laundrie took his own life before he could be brought to justice, the evidence collected during the investigation supports the conclusion that Gabby Petito was a victim of domestic violence and homicide at his hands.

comment_text: On August 12th 2021 in Moab Utah, shortly before Gabby Petito and Brian Laundrie were pulled over by the Moab Utah PD, Brian assaulted Gabby, which had happened while they were in the transit van and while they were at a store. He had slapped and punched her a number of times. While they were on the road that day, the Moab Utah PD noticed the small white transit van they were in, swerving back and forth and then the Moab Utah PD pulled them over shortly afterwards. That all happened in Moab Utah. Then 15 days later, on August 27th 2021 in Bridger-Teton National Park in Wyoming, Brian manually strangled Gabby to death and he beat Gabby to death and left her there. Brian then drove back home in a hurry to tell his parents what he did. Brian then ran off to the Carlton Reserve in Florida near by where he and his parents live, and he committed suicide by shooting himself in the head, to escape Justice for murdering Gabby like the coward he was. Brian will always be known as a domestic abuser and as a coward and as a murderer, who physically abused and murdered Gabby. Rest In Peace Sweet Gabby🦋💜🌻💙🌈❤️🌹.

comment_text:
There are several comments asking what happened, so I’ll throw down a quick version as best I recall- people can fix or edit as I’m not super well versed in it, but just to offer a basis for those who are unfamiliar:

It’s a tragic case where there was a young couple that went on an extended road trip and the boyfriend wound up strangling the poor girl in the woods. It’s conjectured that his family may have tried to cover for him upon his return, but I believe they deny this. He then went on the run and eventually committed suicide. 

As other comments point out, it’s a sad reminder that sometimes people aren’t who they pretend to be… or, perhaps even scarier, they aren’t always who *we* pretend they are.
